notes.curated: The smallest and fastest model in the original Claude 3 family, released March 2024. Designed for near-instant responses on simple tasks; significantly weaker than Claude 3.5 Haiku on complex reasoning and coding. Largely superseded by Claude 3.5 Haiku but useful for cost-sensitive high-volume workloads where 3.5 Haiku pricing is prohibitive. No extended thinking. Arena Elo sits in the lower range (sub-1200) compared to newer models.

Anthropic's fast, low-cost model in the Claude 3.5 series, positioned below Claude 3.5 Sonnet in capability but offering much lower latency and cost. Released late 2024 as a replacement for Claude 3 Haiku. Competitive with GPT-4o-mini class models on instruction following and coding tasks. No extended thinking. Best for high-throughput pipelines, classification, and lightweight agentic tasks. Artificial Analysis ranks it as a strong value-for-money option in its tier.

Anthropic's most advanced model before the Claude 4 generation, released in early 2025. Introduced hybrid reasoning: it can operate in standard mode or switch to an extended thinking mode for hard problems. Exceptionally strong on SWE-bench Verified (49%+) and coding tasks; widely praised on r/LocalLLaMA as the best coding model available at its release. Some users note it can be verbose and over-explains. Arena Elo peaked around 1300, placing it at or near the top of the leaderboard at launch.

The extended-thinking variant of Claude 3.7 Sonnet, accessed via the thinking parameter (budgets up to 128k tokens of internal reasoning). Produces longer, more deliberate outputs on math, science, and complex coding problems. Noticeably better than the standard variant on competition-level math (AIME 2024: ~60–70%) and PhD-level science (GPQA). Latency is substantially higher; not suitable for real-time interactive use. The thinking tokens are not visible in the final output by default.

The entry-level model in the Claude 4.x generation, designed for speed and cost efficiency over raw capability. Best suited for high-throughput classification, summarization, and routing tasks where latency and cost matter most. Falls behind Sonnet and Opus on complex reasoning, long-context synthesis, and agentic multi-step tasks. No extended thinking mode. Positioned at the bottom of the Claude 4 family but significantly faster than larger siblings.

Anthropic's flagship model at the start of the Claude 4 generation, positioned as the most capable model in the family. Excels at complex multi-step reasoning, long-form synthesis, research tasks, and sophisticated agentic behavior. Supports extended thinking mode with notably deeper chain-of-thought than Sonnet. Significantly more expensive per token than Sonnet; primarily used for tasks where quality trumps cost. Arena Elo at launch was in the 1300+ range, competing with GPT-4.5 class models.

First point update to Opus 4, refining alignment, reducing hallucination rates, and improving long-context retrieval accuracy. Maintains the same extended thinking capability as Opus 4 with incremental gains on MMLU-Pro and GPQA benchmarks. Recommended over Opus 4 for production deployments requiring high factual accuracy. Performance improvements are modest but measurable on scientific reasoning tasks.

Substantial update within the Opus 4 lineage, with improved coding ability (particularly on SWE-bench style tasks), better tool-use and function-calling reliability, and refined instruction adherence. Retains extended thinking mode. Community reception on r/LocalLLaMA was positive, noting stronger performance on agentic coding benchmarks versus earlier Opus variants. One of the higher-ranked closed models on Artificial Analysis holistic scores in mid-2025.

Iterative refinement of Opus 4.5 with targeted improvements to reasoning depth and reduced verbosity in long outputs. Extended thinking remains a core feature. Gains on math and formal reasoning benchmarks (AMC, AIME-style problems) noted in community evaluations. Generally interchangeable with Opus 4.5 for most use cases but slightly preferred for mathematical and scientific reasoning workloads.

A latency-optimized variant of Opus 4.6 using speculative decoding or similar inference acceleration techniques to reduce time-to-first-token and throughput latency at some cost to output quality. Intended for interactive applications that need Opus-class quality but cannot tolerate the standard Opus latency. Quality gap versus standard Opus 4.6 is small for most conversational tasks but widens on long extended-thinking chains.

The most recent and capable model in the Claude 4 Opus series as of mid-2025. Incorporates multi-modal reasoning improvements and further reductions in hallucination on factual retrieval tasks. Extended thinking mode reaches the longest reasoning chains in the Anthropic lineup. Community benchmarks place it near the top of closed-model rankings alongside GPT-5 class models. Best choice for the most demanding research, legal, and scientific synthesis tasks.

The initial Sonnet model in the Claude 4 generation, released alongside Opus 4 as the cost-effective counterpart. Strong on coding and instruction following; competitive with GPT-4.1 class models. Supports extended thinking mode. Largely superseded by Sonnet 4.5 and 4.6 for most use cases but remains available for reproducibility. Solid Arena scores in the 1250–1280 Elo range on LMSYS at launch.

Mid-tier model in the Claude 4.x lineup, balancing capability and cost. Excels at coding, instruction following, and structured data tasks; commonly used as the default workhorse in agentic pipelines. Supports extended thinking mode for harder reasoning problems. Competitive with GPT-4.1 on code generation benchmarks but slightly behind the Opus tier on open-ended creative and research tasks. Arena ranking typically sits in the upper-middle tier.

Incremental update to Sonnet 4.5 within the Claude 4 series, incorporating instruction-following refinements and improved tool-use reliability. Performs similarly to Sonnet 4.5 on most benchmarks but with tighter alignment to system-prompt constraints and fewer refusals on edge cases. Supports extended thinking. Generally the recommended Sonnet variant for agentic and tool-heavy production deployments as of mid-2025.

Cohere's flagship enterprise model released early 2025, designed for RAG, tool use, and enterprise agentic workflows. Features a 256k context window and is optimized for structured retrieval and connector-based pipelines (Cohere's native enterprise integrations). Benchmarks competitively with GPT-4-class models on RAG-specific evals. Less well-known in general-purpose community rankings but highly regarded in enterprise search and document-processing contexts. No public extended thinking mode.

The August 2024 refresh of Cohere's Command R model, a mid-sized RAG-focused model with a 128k context window. Optimized for retrieval-augmented generation and multi-step tool use in enterprise pipelines. Weaker than Command R+ on complex reasoning but significantly cheaper and faster. Not a strong general-purpose chat model; shines in structured retrieval pipelines. Largely superseded by Command A for new Cohere deployments.

The August 2024 update to Cohere's largest Command R model, with improved tool use and RAG accuracy over the original Command R+. 128k context window; strong on multi-document synthesis and structured enterprise tasks. Competes with GPT-4-class models on RAG benchmarks. Generally the top Cohere option before Command A launched. Community (r/LocalLLaMA) views it as a solid enterprise RAG model but not a general-purpose frontier model.

Cohere's small 7B-parameter Command R model released December 2024, designed for on-device or low-cost inference while retaining the RAG and tool-use optimizations of the larger Command R family. 128k context. Significantly weaker than Command R or Command R+ on complex tasks but useful for lightweight retrieval pipelines and cost-constrained deployments. Notable as a rare small closed-weight model from Cohere.

DeepSeek-Chat-V3.1 is the chat/instruct deployment of the DeepSeek-V3.1 family, a 671B MoE model (37B active) that supports both general chat and hybrid reasoning modes switchable via system prompt. Architecture is identical to V3.1-Terminus and V3-Base. It is the API-accessible version serving as the backbone for the V3.1 product line.

DeepSeek-R1 is a 671B MoE reasoning model (37B active) trained via GRPO reinforcement learning without a supervised fine-tuning phase before RL, released January 2025. It achieves 79.8% pass@1 on AIME 2024 and 97.3% on MATH-500, matching OpenAI o1-1217 on both. Maximum generation length is 32,768 tokens; sampling uses temperature 0.6, top-p 0.95.

DeepSeek-R1-0528 is a May 2025 updated checkpoint of DeepSeek-R1, described by DeepSeek as a minor version upgrade. AIME 2024 accuracy rose from ~79.8% to 91.4% and Codeforces rating jumped ~400 points to ~1930. The model averages roughly double the chain-of-thought token length (~23K vs ~12K) on hard problems, and hallucination rate and function-calling support are measurably improved over the original.

DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B is a 70B dense model fine-tuned on Llama-3 series weights using reasoning traces generated by DeepSeek-R1, released January 2025. It attains competitive results on AIME 2024, MATH-500, and LiveCodeBench relative to models of similar size. Inference sampling uses temperature 0.6 and top-p 0.95; the model is open-weight under Llama-3's community license.

DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B is a 32B dense model fine-tuned on Qwen2.5-32B weights using reasoning traces from DeepSeek-R1, released January 2025. It outperforms OpenAI-o1-mini across multiple benchmarks and set state-of-the-art results for dense models at its release. Community testers note strong math and coding performance that punches above its size class.

DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp is an experimental September 2025 release that introduced DeepSeek Sparse Attention (DSA) — a fine-grained sparse attention mechanism shared across all query heads — as a stepping stone between V3.1-Terminus and V3.2. Benchmark performance is on par with V3.1-Terminus on both short and long-context tasks, with the architectural goal of reducing inference FLOPs on long sequences. It is publicly released as a research preview rather than a production checkpoint.

DeepSeek-V3.2-Speciale is a high-compute, long-thinking variant of V3.2 augmented with DeepSeek-Math-V2 theorem-proving capabilities, released December 2025. It achieved gold-medal-level results on IOI 2025, ICPC World Final 2025, IMO 2025, and CMO 2025 competition benchmarks. The model does not support tool-calling; it is designed exclusively for deep reasoning tasks.

The stable release of Gemini 2.0 Flash, Google's fast general-purpose model from the 2.0 generation. Natively multimodal (text, image, audio input) with a 1M-token context window. No extended thinking mode (that arrived with 2.5). Strong on coding, summarization, and instruction following for its cost tier. Benchmarks well on LiveBench relative to its price. A reliable workhorse model; superseded by Gemini 2.5 Flash in most use cases but remains available for reproducibility.

The smallest Gemini 2.0 model, designed for edge and high-volume pipelines. Offers very low latency and cost at the expense of reasoning quality. Lacks extended thinking. Suitable for light classification, simple Q&A, and preprocessing steps in larger pipelines. Community reception was positive for latency-sensitive use cases but the model is largely overshadowed by Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite for new projects.

Google's cost-efficient model in the Gemini 2.5 family, offering most of Gemini 2.5 Pro's capability at a fraction of the price. Retains the 1M-token context window and thinking mode (configurable thinking budget). Widely considered the best price-performance closed model in mid-2025; Artificial Analysis ranks it highly on value metrics. Slight quality gap versus 2.5 Pro on hard reasoning but negligible for most production tasks. Very popular on r/singularity for its speed and context length.

The lightest tier in the Gemini 2.5 family, optimized for maximum throughput and minimum cost. Reduced model size compared to Gemini 2.5 Flash; thinking mode may be limited or absent. Best for real-time classification, routing, and extraction tasks where latency is critical. Noticeably weaker on complex multi-step reasoning. Comparable in positioning to GPT-4.1-nano or Gemini 2.0 Flash Lite.

Google's flagship reasoning model in the Gemini 2.x series, featuring a 1M-token context window and built-in chain-of-thought reasoning (thinking tokens). Topped the LMSys Arena leaderboard in early-to-mid 2025 with Elo scores around 1380+, making it the #1 ranked model for a sustained period. Exceptional on long-context tasks, coding (LiveBench top-tier), and multimodal reasoning. Some community complaints about verbose reasoning traces and occasional over-refusals. Pricing reflects its premium position.

Early preview of the Gemini 3 generation's Flash-tier model. As a preview, capabilities and pricing are subject to change; not recommended for stable production workloads. Initial community impressions on r/singularity noted significant coding and reasoning gains over Gemini 2.5 Flash, though benchmarks are preliminary. Thinking mode appears to be included. Context window expected to remain at 1M tokens or larger.

models.display_name: Google: Nano Banana Pro (Gemini 3 Pro Image Preview)
Google: Nano Banana 2 (Gemini 3.1 Flash Image Preview)

notes.curated: The lightweight entry in the Gemini 3.1 preview family, targeting low-latency, cost-sensitive pipelines. Capability improvements over Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite are expected but preliminary benchmarks are sparse. Preview status means stability and API surface may change. Best used for experimenting with next-generation Flash Lite capabilities before stable release.

Preview release of the flagship Pro model in the Gemini 3.1 line. Positions above the Flash previews in raw capability; initial benchmarks suggest top-tier performance on reasoning, coding, and long-context tasks, potentially challenging Claude Opus 4.7 and GPT-5 class models. As a preview it may have rate limits and usage restrictions. Thinking/reasoning mode is included. Community reception has been enthusiastic but cautious given preview status.

models.display_name: Google: Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview Custom Tools

notes.curated: Gemma 3 27B IT is Google's March 2025 instruction-tuned dense model with 27B parameters, 128K context window, and multimodal capability (image+text). It topped several open-model leaderboards at release for its size class, besting Llama 3.3 70B on some reasoning and multilingual benchmarks while using fewer parameters. The r/LocalLLaMA community praised its low refusal rate and sharp instruction following; a common criticism is that 27B is an awkward size — too large for 16 GB single-GPU at FP16 (needs Q4), not quite as capable as 70B class. Vision quality is strong for a sub-30B model.

Gemma 3n E4B IT is the 4B effective-parameter variant in the Gemma 3n Nano family using the MatFormer nested architecture, also announced at Google I/O 2025. It provides a step up in quality over E2B while remaining suitable for on-device deployment on high-end mobile hardware. Multimodal (image+text) capable. Community benchmarks as of mid-2025 place it ahead of Llama 3.2 3B and competitive with Phi-3.5 Mini on reasoning tasks, with a smaller memory footprint than its nominal parameter count suggests.

Gemma 4 31B IT (Instruct-Tuned) is Google's April 2025 flagship open model in the Gemma 4 family, featuring a hybrid dense-MoE architecture with 31B total parameters and a 128K context window with native multimodal support (text + images). On Artificial Analysis it ranks among the top open models, competitive with Llama 4 Maverick on reasoning while being smaller. Community feedback highlights strong multilingual and instruction-following quality with minimal refusals compared to earlier Gemma models. Requires ~20 GB VRAM at BF16; Q4 quants run on a single 16 GB GPU.

The original Mistral Large (February 2024) was Mistral's first flagship closed-access API model, a dense transformer of undisclosed size positioned to compete with GPT-4 class models. It introduced strong multilingual reasoning, native function calling, and JSON output for enterprise workflows. Mistral superseded it with Mistral Large 2 in July 2024, which 'vastly outperformed' this version; the original is largely legacy at this point and rarely benchmarked in isolation.

Mixtral 8x22B Instruct is a sparse MoE model with 141B total parameters and 39B active parameters per token (8 experts per layer, top-2 routing), released April 2024. It was the most capable open-weight model at launch, featuring a 64K context window, native function calling, and multilingual support, while running faster than any dense 70B model due to sparse activation. The AI community widely celebrated it as a breakthrough in open-source cost-performance, and it held the top open-source position until Llama 3 405B arrived.

Mixtral 8x7B Instruct is a sparse Mixture-of-Experts decoder-only model with 46.7B total parameters and 12.9B active parameters per token (8 experts, top-2 routing), released December 2023. It runs at the inference cost of a ~13B model while outperforming Llama 2 70B on most benchmarks with 6x faster inference. Community reception was extremely positive, establishing MoE as the dominant open-source architecture for efficiency; the instruction-tuned variant scored top results on MT-Bench among open models at release.

Kimi-K2 is the base 1T-parameter MoE model from Moonshot AI (32B active, 384 experts, 8+1 shared per token, 61 layers), pretrained on 15.5T tokens with zero training instability using the Muon optimizer. It is available under a Modified MIT License. The base checkpoint underpins both the Instruct and Thinking variants and was the first open-weight model to cross 65% on SWE-bench Verified without test-time scaling.

Kimi-K2-Thinking is a 1T-parameter MoE model (32B active) released by Moonshot AI as a thinking/reasoning agent that reasons step-by-step while invoking tools across 200–300 sequential calls. It sets state-of-the-art on Humanity's Last Exam (HLE with tools: 44.9%), BrowseComp (60.2%), SWE-bench Verified (71.3%), and AIME 2025 (99.1% with Python). The checkpoint is distributed in native INT4 quantization, with a 256K context window.

Kimi-K2.5 is a natively multimodal 1T MoE (32B active) model from Moonshot AI, built via continual pretraining on ~15T mixed visual and text tokens on top of Kimi-K2-Base. Vision integration uses MoonViT (400M parameters), making multimodal understanding architectural rather than bolted-on. The model supports a 256K context window and both instant and thinking inference modes.

Kimi-K2.6 is a 1T MoE (32B active) open-weights model released April 20, 2026 by Moonshot AI, focusing on long-horizon coding and multi-agent orchestration. It supports 300 parallel sub-agents across 4,000 coordinated steps (up from K2.5's 100 agents/1,500 steps) and reduces hallucination rate to 39% vs K2.5's 65% on the AA-Omniscience Index. Scores 96% on τ²-Bench Telecom and achieves Elo 1520 on GDPval-AA agentic evaluation.

models.display_name: Nex AGI: DeepSeek V3.1 Nex N1

notes.curated: Llama 3.3 Nemotron Super 49B v1.5 is a refined iteration of v1 sharing the same NAS-derived 49B architecture from Llama 3.3 70B Instruct, with further post-training improvements targeting reasoning, chat alignment, and agentic workflow quality. Internal benchmarks report MATH500 97.4%, AIME-2024 87.5%, AIME-2025 82.7%, GPQA 72.0%, and LiveCodeBench 73.6%, placing it among the top-performing 49B-class open models. It inherits the single-GPU (H100/H200) deployment profile and 128K context of v1.

Nemotron 3 Nano is a 31.6B-total/3.2B-active (approximately 3.5B active per sources) hybrid Mamba-Transformer MoE model sharing the architectural family of Nemotron 3 Super, released December 2025. It uses interleaved Mamba-2 and MoE layers with 128 experts (plus 1 shared) and 6 active per token alongside 6 attention layers, supports a 1M-token context window, and achieves up to 3.3x higher throughput than comparable models. It supports configurable reasoning traces for both agentic and conversational tasks and is fully open-weight.

Nemotron 3 Super is a 120B-total/12B-active hybrid Latent Mixture-of-Experts model interleaving Mamba-2, LatentMoE, and attention layers, released in 2025 with a 1-million-token context window. Pre-trained on 25 trillion tokens using NVFP4 on NVIDIA Blackwell, it incorporates Multi-Token Prediction layers for improved throughput and quality. It matches or exceeds GPT-OSS 120B on accuracy while delivering 11% higher throughput per B200 GPU and 2.2x higher throughput on standard 8K/16K workloads; designed for complex multi-agent agentic applications like software development and cybersecurity.

Nemotron Nano 12B v2 VL is a 12B multimodal reasoning model pairing the RADIOv2.5/CRadioV2-H vision encoder with the Nemotron Nano v2 12B hybrid Mamba-Transformer language backbone, supporting image, video (MP4/MOV/WEBM), and text inputs with a 128K context. Trained with SFT across 270 datasets (27.7 TB total) covering document, chart, OCR, and video understanding, it achieves leading results on OCRBench v2 and averages approximately 74 across MMMU, MathVista, AI2D, OCRBench, ChartQA, DocVQA, and Video-MME. It is NVIDIA's primary compact vision-language reasoning model as of late 2025.

OpenAI's iterative update to GPT-4o, released April 2025 with a 1M-token context window and significantly improved instruction following and coding ability. Positioned as the workhorse API model before GPT-5's arrival. SWE-bench Verified score of ~55% at release made it one of the strongest coding models available. Cheaper than GPT-4o at comparable quality; very well received by developers. Comes in three sizes: gpt-4.1, gpt-4.1-mini, and gpt-4.1-nano.

OpenAI's fifth-generation flagship model, released in mid-2025. Unified architecture that merges reasoning and standard generation in a single model (replacing the separate o-series for most use cases). Tops most major benchmarks including MMLU-Pro, GPQA, and competition math at release; claimed Arena Elo above 1400 in early evaluations. Exceptionally strong on agentic tasks, multimodal reasoning, and code generation. Community reception was highly positive with particular praise for long-context coherence and reduced hallucination rates.

The cost-efficient smaller variant of GPT-5, positioned similarly to how GPT-4o-mini related to GPT-4o. Retains much of GPT-5's instruction-following and coding quality at a fraction of the cost and latency. No separate extended thinking mode (the reasoning integration is internal). Excellent for high-throughput production pipelines. Community considers it one of the best small closed models, competing closely with Gemini 2.5 Flash on price-performance.

GPT-OSS 120B is OpenAI's first fully open-weight model release since GPT-2, a sparse MoE transformer with 117B total parameters and 5.1B active parameters per token (128 experts, top-4 routing, 36 layers), released August 2025 under Apache 2.0. It uses alternating dense and locally banded sparse attention with grouped multi-query attention (group size 8), RoPE positional encoding, a 128K context window, and was trained on a mostly English STEM/coding dataset using o200k_harmony tokenizer with RL informed by o3 and other frontier systems. It achieves near-parity with OpenAI o4-mini on core reasoning benchmarks while fitting on a single 80GB GPU (H100 or AMD MI300X).

GPT-OSS 20B is the smaller of OpenAI's open-weight pair, a sparse MoE transformer with 21B total parameters and 3.6B active parameters per token, released alongside GPT-OSS 120B in August 2025 under Apache 2.0. It shares the same MoE/attention/RoPE architecture as the 120B sibling but is designed for lower-latency and on-device or edge use cases, runnable with as little as 16GB of memory. Benchmarked against OpenAI's own o3-mini, it delivers comparable results on common reasoning and coding tasks and is regarded as a strong small open-weight reasoning model for local deployment.

OpenAI's first publicly available reasoning model (full release December 2024 after a preview period), using internal chain-of-thought to tackle hard reasoning tasks before producing output. Excels at competition math, PhD-level science, and formal logic. Slower and more expensive than GPT-4o; thinking tokens are hidden from the output. Largely superseded by o3 for the hardest tasks, but remains available and cost-effective for medium-difficulty reasoning. Arena Elo at launch was top-3 globally.

OpenAI's most powerful reasoning model at the time of its release (April 2025), achieving frontier performance on ARC-AGI (87.5% in high-compute mode), AIME 2024, and GPQA Diamond. Uses extended chain-of-thought reasoning with a configurable compute budget. Significantly more expensive and slower than GPT-4.1; intended for research, scientific, and hard mathematical tasks where quality is paramount. Community reception was exceptional; widely viewed as a breakthrough on AGI benchmarks. Arena Elo was among the highest at release.

Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct is the instruction-tuned variant of the Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B series, matching performance of Qwen3-235B-A22B-Instruct-2507 on certain benchmarks while offering significant advantages on ultra-long-context tasks up to 256K tokens. Architecture uses Gated DeltaNet + Gated Attention hybrid, extreme-sparsity MoE, and Multi-Token Prediction for inference speed. Qwen3-Coder-Next is built on top of this base.

Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Thinking is an 80B MoE model with only 3B active parameters, using a hybrid architecture combining Gated DeltaNet and Gated Attention with high-sparsity MoE and Multi-Token Prediction. It supports context lengths up to 256K tokens. On complex reasoning benchmarks it surpasses Qwen3-30B-A3B-Thinking-2507, Qwen3-32B-Thinking, and Gemini-2.5-Flash-Thinking across multiple evals despite having far fewer active parameters.

Qwen3.5-397B-A17B is the flagship Qwen3.5 model at 397B total parameters and 17B active, released February 16, 2026. It scores 83.6 on LiveCodeBench v6 and 91.3 on AIME26. The Qwen3.5 architecture introduces a Gated Delta Network + sparse MoE hybrid with expanded 250K vocabulary. Community benchmarks show it competitive with top-tier proprietary models on math and coding.

QwQ-32B is a 32B dense reasoning model released March 2025, trained primarily via reinforcement learning with continuous RL scaling demonstrated to close the gap to much larger MoE models. It scores 86% on AIME 2024 and 59% on GPQA Diamond, competing with DeepSeek-R1 and o1-mini on math and science benchmarks. Available open-weight under Apache 2.0; LiveBench shows it landing between R1 and o3-mini at a fraction of inference cost.

xAI's fourth-generation flagship model, positioned as a direct competitor to GPT-5 and Claude Opus 4.x. Integrated with real-time X (Twitter) data access, giving it a unique advantage on current-events questions. Strong on math and coding in community evaluations; xAI claims top-tier performance on AIME and GPQA. Extended thinking/reasoning mode is available. Some community skepticism about benchmark reproducibility, but general reception has been positive particularly for its knowledge currency.
